summary: Expanded the candidate matrix with five official-source rows focused on RAG/LLM evaluation platforms: Langfuse, Opik, TruLens, MLflow GenAI evaluation, and Giskard. Captured concrete evidence for trace scoring, RAG-specific metrics, datasets/experiments, CI hooks, human feedback, production monitoring, and RAG test suites.
next_angle: Cover the remaining official-source candidates from the work order: Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex evaluators, Haystack evaluation, and OpenAI Evals; then convert all rows into a compact workbook schema with scoring weights.
open_questions: Should framework-native evaluators such as LlamaIndex and Haystack be primary matrix rows, or listed as framework-fit caveats unless the target stack already uses them?
Should OpenAI Evals be treated as a primary candidate for application/system evals or as a caveat row better suited to OpenAI/model-centric benchmarking?
Which deployment and governance attributes need verification before scoring commercial or managed options: OSS license, self-hosting, SaaS availability, RBAC, data retention, or enterprise controls?

sources.title: Evaluation of RAG pipelines with Ragas - Langfuse
sources.note: Official Langfuse cookbook showing trace/span scoring and Ragas-based RAG evaluation over production traces.

sources.title: comet-ml/opik GitHub repository
sources.note: Official Opik repository describing observability, automated evaluation, datasets, experiments, RAG metrics, PyTest CI integration, cloud, and self-hosting.

sources.title: The RAG Triad - TruLens
sources.note: Official TruLens concept page defining context relevance, groundedness, and answer relevance for RAG evaluation.

sources.title: Evaluating LLMs and Agents with MLflow
sources.note: Official MLflow GenAI evaluation documentation covering datasets, human feedback, LLM-as-judge scorers, systematic evaluation, and production monitoring.

sources.title: RAG Evaluation - Giskard Documentation
sources.note: Official Giskard open-source RAG evaluation example covering retrieval quality, groundedness, answer quality, out-of-scope questions, and multi-turn RAG tests.

findings.text: Langfuse is a strong primary-row candidate for teams that want observability-first RAG evaluation: its official cookbook shows scores attached to traces/spans, segmentation and analytics over scores, and Ragas evaluation over production traces without requiring ground-truth answers.

Opik is a strong primary-row candidate for a production engineering matrix because the official repository documents tracing, datasets and experiments, LLM-as-judge metrics for hallucination/moderation/RAG assessment, PyTest CI integration, production dashboards, online evaluation rules, cloud use, and self-hosting.

TruLens should be scored highly for RAG-specific evaluation concepts: its RAG Triad explicitly separates context relevance, groundedness, and answer relevance, which maps directly to retrieval, grounding, and final-answer quality dimensions in the workbook.

MLflow GenAI evaluation is a primary-row candidate for teams already standardizing on MLflow or needing broader AI engineering lifecycle support: official docs list evaluation datasets, human feedback, LLM-as-a-judge scorers, systematic evaluation, tracing, and production monitoring.

Giskard is a primary-row candidate for test-suite-driven RAG validation: official docs show a comprehensive RAG test suite spanning retrieval quality, groundedness, answer quality, out-of-scope questions, and multi-turn conversational RAG.

A fair public-source scoring model should separate capability from packaging: proposed dimensions are RAG-specific metric coverage, repeatable regression/CI workflow, trace/observability integration, human review and feedback workflow, deployment/governance fit, integration maturity, and evidence maturity.

The candidate list is not yet complete for the requested workbook: Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and OpenAI Evals still need official-source evidence and classification before analysis/synthesis.

adequacy.goal_alignment: This search iteration advances the goal by adding five publicly verifiable candidate rows and provisional scoring dimensions, but the research set is not yet complete enough for finalization.
adequacy.coverage_summary: Covered Langfuse, Opik, TruLens, MLflow GenAI evaluation, and Giskard from official or project-owned sources. Not yet covered in this iteration: Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and OpenAI Evals.

covered_requirements.requirement: Use current public sources and prefer official documentation, GitHub repositories, release docs, and vendor docs over blog summaries.
covered_requirements.evidence: All five sources are official docs, project-owned docs, or official GitHub repositories.

covered_requirements.requirement: Compare open-source and commercially relevant tools through publicly verifiable evidence links.
covered_requirements.evidence: Added evidence-linked findings for OSS/commercially relevant candidates: Langfuse, Opik, TruLens, MLflow, and Giskard.

covered_requirements.requirement: Shape research toward a decision matrix for production RAG evaluation.
covered_requirements.evidence: Captured production-relevant dimensions: RAG metrics, CI/regression, observability, human feedback, governance/deployment, integrations, and evidence maturity.

coverage_gaps.gap: Remaining candidate tools from the work order still need official evidence and primary-row versus caveat classification.
No workbook schema or scoring weights have been built yet.

evidence_risks.risk: Deployment/licensing/governance claims for some candidates may require separate verification from licensing, pricing, or deployment docs before scoring.

adequacy.recommended_next_angle: Gather official evidence for Evidently, Weave, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and OpenAI Evals, then classify framework-specific and model-benchmark tools as primary rows or caveats.

validation_evidence.reason: The findings directly support the tooling matrix, but they are not a full candidate set yet.
No private paths, personal data, contact data, tokens, or local-only tool names are included in the user-facing findings.
Sources cover five distinct vendors/projects and are official or project-owned.
Search should continue because several named candidates remain uncovered.
